parts.content:
_CBR Bank_ holds a large amount of information on its customers. Some customers are concerned about the security, privacy and anonymity of their data.

For **each** of the concerns above, explain**one** policy that _CBR Bank_ could use to address the concerns of its customers.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Security:**

*   User access to data is limited to authorized personnel – to ensure data is secure during storage.
*   Username and password access is implemented – to ensure data is secure during storage.
*   A password policy is implemented (e.g., minimum length, mix of characters, changed after a given number of days etc_._).
*   Two-factor / two-step authentication is implemented (e.g., one-time password/PIN to a mobile phone, code-generating device supplied by the bank, confirmation email).
*   Data is encrypted – to ensure data is secure during transmission.
*   A firewall is used to protect the bank’s server.
*   Bank servers are kept updated with the latest software / security patches.
*   Bank employees are prohibited from accessing customer data on personal devices / devices outside the company network – to ensure that all devices are protected by the bank’s security measures.

**Privacy:**

*   Customers are informed to specify how the data may be used – if/how it may be shared with third parties.
*   Only authorized personnel will have access to the bank database – not all employees will be able to view data.

**Anonymity:**

*   Ensure the customer’s anonymity is maintained – when data is shared with third parties, the data that could give the identity of a customer must be detached.
*   Reports are kept anonymous – reports cannot allow individuals to be identified.

**_N.B.:_** _T__he response requires an explanation of a policy and not a discussion of the problems themselves. There must be a policy for **each** kind of concern: security, privacy and anonymity and reason(s)._

_Award **\[1\]** for identifying a policy that CBR Bank could use to address the security, privacy and anonymity concerns of its customers and **\[1\]** for a development of the policy identified up to a maximum of **\[2\]**._

_Mark as **\[2\] + \[2\] + \[2\]**._

parts.content:
The chief executive officer (CEO) of _CBR Bank_, Alice McEwan, said in a recent interview, “_CBR Bank_ will be replacing all passwords, PINs and personal verification questions for our online banking and mobile banking with voice biometrics recognition.”

To what extent are the changes proposed by Alice beneficial for both _CBR Bank_’s customers **and** _CBR Bank_’s IT support?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**For customers:**

**Advantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   Customers don’t have to remember a password or PIN code / may not need additional verification such as one-time codes (automation).
*   It is more secure, as voice characteristics are unique.
*   It is harder for others to hack online banking with voice biometrics recognition (security).
*   Some customers may have physical conditions that make entering PINs/passwords difficult – voice recognition will avoid having to type (access/inclusion).

**Disadvantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   The voice recognition system may not accept foreign accents or a range of voices (systems).
*   Illness (such as a cold) can change a person’s voice, making identification difficult (systems).
*   A person’s voice can be easily recorded and used for unauthorized access (security).
*   Someone with very similar voices (e.g., a member of the same family) may be able to gain access to the bank account (security).

**For IT support:**

**Advantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   It is more secure, less likely to be hacked – fewer problems for IT staff to deal with (automation, systems).
*   It is easy for customers to record by themselves – no IT staff required to set up (automation, systems).
*   IT staff do not have to deal with lost password/PIN (automation, systems).

**Disadvantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   The voice recognition system may not accept particular accents – customers cannot access their online banking and will need support (systems).
*   A person’s voice can be easily recorded and used for unauthorized access – customers may complain about unauthorized access, IT staff will have to investigate hacked accounts (security, values).
*   Illness (such as a cold) can change a person’s voice, making identification difficult – a greater number of customers might need to call support to access their own account (systems).
*   When the new system is implemented, _CBR_ _Bank_’s IT support could be overwhelmed with overlooked bugs (reliability).
*   Audio files / biometric templates will require more storage space than passwords / PINs – this may make backing up data more time consuming / require IT support to increase available storage space, etc (data).
*   Initial implementation of the new system may require additional IT support staff.
*   IT support staff may face an increased workload (e.g., if the old system initially has to run parallel to the new system).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** security, authentication, stakeholder, reliability, data, security, change, identity, power, systems, values, ethics, accountability, transparency, access, inclusion

parts.content:
**A cashless society**

In the near future, it is possible that cash will not be accepted as a means of payment in Sweden. People are already using alternative ways of paying, such as mobile payment, card payment and internet payment. Currently, over 95% of citizens in Sweden have internet access.

Many people in Sweden claim there are advantages of using an app developed by Swish. The Swish app allows friends to share a restaurant bill, pay where credit or debit cards are not accepted, for babysitting or parking tickets, or make a donation at church.

However, other people in Sweden claim that making the Swish app the only means of payment may increase inequalities within the country.

Discuss whether countries should pass legislation making apps such as Swish the only means of payment.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Benefits of apps such as Swish _being_ the only form of payment (claim)**

*   No need to carry cash (digitalization) / credit cards – no risk of being stolen/no risk of not having enough cash.
*   No risk of credit card being used fraudulently (values).
*   Transactions are recorded – there is proof of payment, and the system should increase transparency / acceptability.
*   Payments are made immediately – no need to wait until person has time to go to bank to get cash (feasibility).
*   Can solve other problems regarding money – bills can be shared, one person pays, and money is transferred (feasibility).
*   In an emergency, money can be transferred to dependents without them being close (e.g., children at university) (feasibility).
*   Allows money transactions between individuals.
*   Can limit the amount of money to be transferred, thus preventing individuals spending more than they have available (acceptability / feasibility).
*   Many people are already used to the app, so it would be a good choice if the country were going cashless (acceptability).
*   Easier for users to track budgets / spending, as all transactions are digital (transparency).

**Disadvantages of apps such as Swish _being_ the only form of payment (counter-claim)**

*   Swedish banks will be able to obtain more data on their users’ transaction habits (privacy concern, ethics, values).
*   Is not available to people who do not have a bank account, so a potential digital divide concern (equity).
*   May be problematic for tourists who may not have the app or who cannot link the purchase to their bank account (systems, equity).
*   Removes the anonymity of the payee in transactions – the app may store a user’s transaction history. This would include date, item, recipient of the money and cost of the item (values, ethics).
*   The bank controls (power) the maximum amount of money that can be transferred, which may limit a person’s spending and may not be appropriate in certain situations (acceptability).
*   It may not be technically possible to make the transition from a society that uses cash for transactions to one that does not (change, systems).
*   If a single app is used, it would give Swish an unfair monopoly over the technology (power, values, ethics).
*   Digital divide – smartphone ownership and use by mature adults.
*   If a person loses their phone, breaks it, or its battery runs out, they have no way to pay for anything (systems, equity).
*   Failure in / lack of phone network coverage could affect when and where people could use the app (systems, equity).
*   Failure of the system / technical issues / down time would prevent people from making transactions (systems, feasibility).

_Accept implicit and explicit references to apps such as the Swish app._

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** business, families, digital divide, access, inclusion, acceptability, feasibility, equity, digitalization, anonymity, privacy, change, power, systems, values, ethics

parts.content: Discuss the implications of AI-driven automation on employment, focusing on the balance between economic efficiency and potential job displacement.
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of AI-driven automation and its general effect on employment.
- Limited discussion on economic efficiency or job displacement, with little depth or balance between the two.
- Few examples or specifics to illustrate key points.


"AI-driven automation can lead to job loss in some industries."
"Automation can make work faster and more efficient."
</commonMistake>

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear explanation of AI-driven automation's dual impact on employment, including both potential economic efficiency and job displacement.
- Some discussion of benefits (e.g., improved productivity and cost reduction) alongside basic mention of risks (e.g., job loss in specific sectors).
- Examples provided to support points, though may lack depth or variety.


"AI-driven automation increases economic efficiency by speeding up processes and reducing costs, which benefits businesses."
"However, certain jobs, especially in manufacturing and repetitive tasks, may be at risk of displacement."
Examples might include references to automation in retail (self-checkout systems) or manufacturing (robotic assembly lines).
</commonMistake>

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- In-depth evaluation of the implications of AI-driven automation on employment, considering both economic efficiency and job displacement.
- Balanced analysis of benefits and risks, showing nuanced understanding of how automation can drive economic growth but also result in challenges for workforce adaptation.
- Detailed examination of sectors where automation is most impactful (e.g., manufacturing, retail, transportation) and where new opportunities may emerge (e.g., tech support, AI maintenance).
- Examples used effectively to illustrate points, with insights into both the positive impacts (e.g., efficiency, growth in new sectors) and negative impacts (e.g., job displacement, need for reskilling).

$$
\text{Economic Efficiency} \leftrightarrow \text{Job Displacement}
$$


"AI-driven automation enhances economic efficiency by reducing labor costs and increasing productivity, allowing companies to produce goods and services at lower prices, potentially increasing consumer access."
"However, automation can displace jobs in sectors where repetitive tasks are common. For instance, autonomous vehicles could reduce the demand for truck drivers, and robotic process automation could decrease the need for administrative roles."
"There is also a shift in job creation, with new roles emerging in AI development, maintenance, and oversight, but this requires significant retraining and may not offset losses in low-skill jobs."
</commonMistake>

<examTip>Balancing these effects is difficult, as job displacement impacts socio-economic stability. Policymakers might need to consider interventions such as retraining programs, universal basic income, or support for sectors heavily affected by automation.</examTip>

parts.content:
**App or intuition?**

Apps have been developed that allow a customer to submit a trip request to a taxi company on their smartphone. These apps allow the customer to select the starting point and final destination of the journey as well as add other information, such as the time of journey and number of passengers.

The developers of the taxi journey app claim it can capture the real-time details of a journey, including the starting point, route, distance travelled, the speed of the car and the final destination. The app integrates this information with real-time traffic information, such as closed roads or accidents. Fares can be estimated before the journey, and the customer can pay digitally with their credit card or by PayPal.

Some traditional taxi drivers in cities such as Paris and Rio de Janeiro have campaigned against the introduction of these apps in taxi companies. Based on previous journeys recorded by the app, the taxi company recommends that their taxi drivers rely on the taxi journey app rather than their own knowledge of the city when determining the best route.

Discuss whether the taxi driver should rely on the taxi journey app, rather than their own knowledge of the city, when determining the best route.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Rely on the taxi journey app (claim)**

*   It will speed up the process of drivers being able to learn the routes (automation), meaning they are able to provide a better service to customers more quickly.
*   The app will give consistency in the routes used, so the passengers should be confident that the route being selected is the most appropriate (trust, transparency).
*   If the decisions of the route in the app are collected, the results may be analysed and the reliability evaluated/increased. It may make it possible to monitor the drivers more closely.
*   The app may be updated in real time, so the driver may not need to check on changes such as roads being closed or reopening (change).
*   The app may result in shorter distances being travelled and cost savings for the taxi company (cost).
*   The driver may not have sufficient knowledge of these areas to be able to determine an effective route.

**Rely on driver’s knowledge and intuition (counter-claim)**

*   They may use their professional judgement / intuition that has been gained by doing the job for a number of years.
*   The system may not be able to determine certain routes, such as those that go outside of the central part of the city. This may mean the usability / effectiveness / reliability of the app is reduced.
*   There may be new requirements that have not been incorporated into the system (change) that may mean it is not providing the most up-to-date information (cost).
*   The route-finding algorithms that exist within the digital system may have inherent/subconscious biases (power, values, ethics). For example, they may route the driver past certain locations, or avoid others.
*   There may be parts of the city where the connection cannot be maintained, such as where there are very tall buildings surrounding the road. This may lead to the app not performing as expected (reliability, feasibility).
*   The taxi driver may feel that they are being surveilled (power, values, ethics).
*   Would the installation of the app be a requirement for the job (systems, ethics, values)?

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:**change, power, systems, values, ethics, apps, security, privacy, algorithms, transparency, reliability, surveillance, cost, feasibility

parts.content: Describe two uses of machine learning.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>2 marks</marks> for each valid use of machine learning described:

- Machine learning can be used for image recognition/computer vision applications, such as facial recognition, object detection, self-driving cars, etc.

- Machine learning can be used for natural language processing (NLP) applications, such as language translation, text summarization, sentiment analysis, etc.

- Machine learning can be used for predictive analytics and forecasting, such as predicting consumer behavior, stock market trends, weather forecasting, etc.

- Machine learning can be used for recommendation systems, such as product recommendations on e-commerce websites, movie/music recommendations, etc.

- Machine learning can be used for fraud detection, such as detecting credit card fraud, cybersecurity threats, etc.

- Machine learning can be used for medical diagnosis and treatment, such as analyzing medical images, predicting disease risk, drug discovery, etc.

parts.content: Distinguish between two types of machine learning.
parts.markscheme:
There are two main types of machine learning:

### Supervised Learning
- Involves training a model on labeled data $\bullet$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The model learns to map input data to known output labels/values $\bullet$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Examples: classification (predicting categories) and regression (predicting numerical values) $\bullet$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Unsupervised Learning
- Involves training a model on unlabeled data $\bullet$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The model tries to find inherent patterns or structures in the data $\bullet$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Examples: clustering (grouping similar data points) and dimensionality reduction $\bullet$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

This question requires an extended response analyzing the positive impacts of artificial intelligence (AI) in a chosen context. Here is a possible markscheme:

- Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a relevant context where AI has been applied (e.g. healthcare, transportation, education, etc.)

- Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks> for well-explained positive impacts of AI in the chosen context, such as:
    - Improved efficiency/productivity
    - Enhanced decision making
    - Automation of repetitive tasks

    - Early detection/diagnosis of diseases
    - Safer transportation systems

- Award up to <marks>2 marks</marks> for considering potential negative impacts or limitations of AI in the chosen context, such as:
    - Job displacement
    - Privacy/security concerns
    - Bias in algorithms
    - Lack of human oversight
    - High costs of implementation
    - Ethical concerns (e.g. autonomous weapons)

- Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a well-supported conclusion evaluating the extent to which AI has made a positive impact in the chosen context.

- Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a clear, well-structured response with appropriate examples to illustrate the points made.

<note>Maximum 6 marks if only positive impacts are discussed without considering potential negative impacts or limitations.</note>

parts.content: To what extent should transparency be a priority in AI-driven decision-making, particularly in high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice?
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of transparency in AI.
- Limited discussion on why transparency might be important, with minimal reference to high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice.
- Few or no examples to support points.

Example Points:

- "Transparency is needed to see how AI decisions are made."
- "It is important in high-stakes areas like finance."

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear explanation of the concept of transparency and its importance in AI decision-making.
- Some discussion of high-stakes areas (finance, criminal justice), mentioning the potential consequences of opaque AI systems.
- Basic mention of risks in not prioritizing transparency, such as accountability and fairness issues.
- Some examples to illustrate key points, though may lack depth.

Example Points:

- "Transparency in AI is crucial for accountability, especially in areas like criminal justice where lives can be directly affected."
- "In finance, a lack of transparency could lead to unfair credit decisions, impacting people's financial stability."
- Examples may include basic references to credit scoring or criminal sentencing decisions influenced by AI without deep analysis.

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- In-depth evaluation of the necessity of transparency in AI, particularly in high-stakes fields.
- Comprehensive discussion of ethical and practical implications, including how transparency supports accountability, trust, and fairness.
- Detailed analysis of risks associated with opaque AI systems, such as potential bias, wrongful decisions, and the challenges to correcting errors without transparency.
- Balanced argument with consideration of challenges to implementing transparency, like trade-offs with proprietary information or the complexity of AI models.
- Use of specific examples to illustrate implications in both finance (e.g., credit scoring or loan approvals) and criminal justice (e.g., sentencing algorithms or predictive policing).

Example Points:

- "Transparency in AI decision-making is essential in high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice, where decisions have far-reaching impacts on individuals' lives. It ensures that affected parties can understand, contest, and seek recourse for decisions that impact them."
- "In criminal justice, lack of transparency in AI-driven sentencing algorithms can lead to biased outcomes without accountability, eroding public trust. Transparency is needed to scrutinize whether the system perpetuates existing biases, particularly against marginalized groups."
- "In finance, opaque algorithms used in credit scoring can disadvantage certain applicants without providing a clear reason, limiting access to financial opportunities."
- <note>Challenges: Achieving transparency can be difficult when models are complex or proprietary, but efforts like explainable AI and regulatory oversight could help balance transparency with innovation.</note>

parts.content: Identify two dilemmas associated with robots and autonomous technologies.
parts.markscheme:
### Dilemma 1
The uncanny valley effect, where people feel discomfort around robots that appear almost human, can affect social acceptance of robots in customer service and healthcare roles. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Dilemma 2

parts.content: Outline the concept of the uncanny valley and its effect on user acceptance.
parts.markscheme:
The uncanny valley refers to the phenomenon where robots or computer animations that appear almost human can cause feelings of unease or revulsion in observers. As the appearance and motion of robots become more human-like, observers' emotional response becomes increasingly positive.
However, at a point where the robot/animation is close to being human-like, but not quite there, observers experience an unsettling feeling, causing their emotional response to drop sharply.
<marks>2 marks</marks>

This effect can negatively impact user acceptance of robots in roles that require close human interaction, such as customer service or healthcare. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Clearly explain the concept of the uncanny valley and its impact on user acceptance to score full marks.</examTip>
<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the social and economic impacts of job displacement due to robots, considering both productivity gains and the need for workforce adaptation.
parts.markscheme:
### Potential Positive Impacts

- Increased productivity and economic growth <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Robots can work 24/7 without breaks, increasing output
  - Lower labor costs can make businesses more competitive

- Creation of new job opportunities <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - New roles in robot maintenance, programming, etc.
  - Workforce can transition to emerging tech sectors

### Potential Negative Impacts

- Job losses and unemployment <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Robots can replace human workers in manufacturing, logistics, etc.
  - Leads to economic hardship for displaced workers

- Inequality and wage stagnation <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Workers with skills complementary to robots benefit
  - But low-skill workers face competition, downward pressure on wages

- Social unrest and resistance <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Fears of job insecurity can fuel backlash against automation
  - Calls for policies to protect human labor

### Workforce Adaptation Needs

- Reskilling and retraining programs <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Equip workers with skills for new roles in tech-driven economy
  - Promote lifelong learning and adaptability

- Social safety nets and transition assistance <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Income support, job placement services for displaced workers
  - Facilitate workforce mobility across sectors

<note>Candidates should consider both economic benefits of productivity gains as well as social costs of job displacement. A balanced evaluation weighing pros and cons is expected for full marks.</note>

questions.specification:
**Technology disruption in Orams Academy**

Many schools use virtual learning environments (VLEs), such as Firefly, PowerSchool Learning, Schoology, Final Site LMS, and Google Classroom. The VLE is used for document storage, lesson content, and homework management as well as for communication between the school, students and parents.

In addition to the VLE, collaborative cloud-based online tools, such as Google G Suite and Microsoft Office 365, are used by students and teachers.

Recently, Daniela Stark, the principal at Orams Academy, has become concerned that using only one VLE may not prove to be as beneficial as originally thought. She has heard that some teachers and students are also successfully using social media tools, such as Facebook, Twitter and Instagram, to share their learning. She has also been told that the lack of bandwidth that the school experiences at times can cause problems.

Daniela is considering an alternative approach, allowing teachers and students to choose the most appropriate tools to manage their learning.

The increasing use of cloud-based applications and online collaborative tools has led to Orams Academy introducing an acceptableuse policy for students and teachers.

Explain**three** reasons why Orams Academy decided to introduce an acceptable use policy.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

*   To establish a philosophy of Orams Academy relating to the use of online environments…
*   which acts as a guiding statement and provides the overarching principles for online behaviour (values).  
    

*   To establish a code of conduct (values)…
*   that provides clear and unambiguous directions about how staff and students should act while online / using appropriate language in text chats / avoiding bullying behaviour / avoiding copying or plagiarism.  
      
    

*   To outline sanctions…
*   that would be imposed if staff or students did not adhere to the code of conduct (values).  
      
    

*   To establish a disclaimer…
*   so that Orams Academy is not responsible for the actions of the staff or students if an illegal activity is carried out / managers at Orams Academy are able to supervise everything that is posted onto the VLE / managers at Orams Academy would be able to read students’ messages (spaces).  
      
    

*   To establish data privacy and protection guidelines (security)…
*   so it will be transparent who owns the data created in the VLE, for how long, and what type of data is collected by the VLE servers (systems).

_Award **\[1\]** for each reason given and an additional **\[1\]** for a development of this reason up to a maximum of **\[2\]** marks._

parts.content: Discuss whether Daniela should make every teacher at Orams Academy use the same learning platform **or** allow each teacher to choose their own preferred learning management approach.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**A single learning platform:**

*   This means that all of the information is centralized and can be easily accessed by staff, students and parents (systems).
*   The training requirements for staff are reduced, as they only have to use one system (systems).
*   It prevents the use of a range of formats that may be incompatible, meaning that resources cannot be shared, or may need adapting to work on a different platform (systems).
*   Having a common learning platform would make it convenient for the IT department to take backup/restore and roll over data to new academic year (systems).
*   It makes it easier for senior managers to monitor the actions of staff, view lessons, etc., for consistency (values, power).
*   Can be integrated into any acceptable use policy or training requirements (values).
*   May be costly and based on proprietary software, meaning that fixes could be expensive and take a long time to happen (change).
*   If the single platform is inaccessible for any reason, all learning content would be inaccessible (systems).

**Teachers choosing their own preferred learning management approach:**

*   allows teachers to use their expertise of particular tools
*   allows teachers to feel they are being allowed to use their creativity and innovative approach to the teaching and learning (power)
*   may integrate with social media tools that may be blocked by the school and provide a better user experience
*   may provide better functionality than a “one size fits all” approach
*   may lead to staff or students working outside an acceptable use policy / using the sites inappropriately
*   the training requirements for staff will be increased if the staff are unfamiliar with the platforms / training will only occur on an _ad hoc_ basis / training might be decreased since staff are already familiar with the tools they choose to use
*   expertise of a platform may depend on particular teachers – if they leave, then no one may be able to support students with this tool (systems).

**Keywords:** business, learning, education, training, judgement, platform, monitoring, acceptable use policy, interoperability, policy, change, systems, values

questions.specification:
**Source A**

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6e966c72-39aa-4aa4-ab61-a0acd131033b)

**Source B (excerpt from article on UniSphere)** 

UniSphere introduced an optional “Verified Student” badge for discussion forums, group projects, and peer tutoring. Verification requires a current student ID number and a selfie that matches the ID photo on file. UniSphere says the badge aims to reduce impersonation and improve trust in academic collaboration. Students may still use a display name in most spaces, but the platform’s support team can view the legal name linked to the account when resolving disputes. UniSphere encourages students to keep profile details “consistent and accurate,” and notes that “misleading identities may result in restrictions.” The platform allows users to add pronouns and language preferences, but warns that some external tools (such as plagiarism checks and video-conferencing add-ons) may not display these fields reliably.

**Source C (excerpt from UniSphere statistics)**

* 74% of students use a display name that differs from their legal name in at least one way (spacing, order, shortened form, or chosen name).  
* 22% of first-year students attempt verification; of these, 9% receive a “photo mismatch” flag and must appeal.

* The most common help-ticket category is “name appears differently across tools” (31% of identity-related tickets).

* In student surveys, 48% say the verified badge increases trust in group work; 37% say it creates a “two-tier” feeling in discussions.

* Course staff report fewer obvious impersonation incidents, but more disputes about who controls a group’s shared account for team submissions.

**Source D (excerpt from blog)**

UniSphere keeps calling the badge “optional,” but it doesn’t feel optional when the most valuable spaces (such as peer tutoring, high-demand project groups, and some course channels) treat verification as the default sign of legitimacy. The platform turns identity into a gatekeeping mechanism: the closer you move toward opportunities, the more you must fit a narrow template of what a “real student” looks like. Identity is not static. 

People change names, photos, language use, and how they present themselves depending on context. Yet UniSphere’s design assumes consistency is the same as honesty. Worse, it quietly re-centres the legal name as the “true” identity because support staff can always see it. That may solve administrative problems, but it can also expose students to unwanted disclosure. If a digital society is supposed to expand participation, systems like this risk shrinking it by making recognition conditional on compliance with a platform’s preferred identity format.

parts.content: Outline two ways that Source A suggests identity on UniSphere can differ across contexts.
parts.markscheme:
**Outline (Total <marks>2 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Different names used in different spaces, such as a display name in one area and another identifier shown elsewhere. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Different profile elements presented depending on the context, such as an avatar or photo in some areas and a legal or ID photo in others. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Different roles or affiliations shown depending on the context, such as a course group identity distinct from a forum identity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Different visibility or audience settings affecting identity presentation, such as a public-facing profile compared to a restricted view. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each clearly outlined way that identity differs across contexts, up to a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Award <marks>0 marks</marks> for generic responses such as people act differently online that do not identify specific contextual differences in identity presentation.</note>


**Sample answer**


Identity on UniSphere can differ across contexts in several ways. Users may use different names in different spaces, such as a display name in some areas while another identifier is shown elsewhere.  

Profile elements can also change depending on context, with an avatar or informal photo used in some spaces and a legal or ID photo required in others. These examples show how identity presentation adapts to different platform settings and audiences.

parts.content: Suggest two changes UniSphere could make to its verification approach in Source B to better reflect the idea that identity is dynamic.
parts.markscheme:
**Suggest (Total <marks>4 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Allow multiple display names with context-specific display rules, such as a chosen name for forums and a legal name for official disputes, recognising that people use different names in different situations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Add an identity history or update workflow for name and photo changes without using punitive language, acknowledging that identity presentation changes over time. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Provide alternative verification methods such as staff attestation or video verification that recognise photos and documents change, reflecting identity as non-static. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Make verification badges more granular or time-limited so verification can be renewed, recognising that identity and circumstances evolve. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Ensure pronouns and language preferences are portable across tools or clearly labelled when not displayed, supporting context-sensitive identity expression. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Create privacy controls that limit support staff access to legal names except when necessary, reducing the re-centring of fixed legal identity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Allow names and photos to change without suspicion, acknowledging that people present themselves differently across contexts. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Avoid treating inconsistency as dishonesty by designing systems that recognise identity as dynamic. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Accommodate changes in language use and self-presentation over time and across spaces. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Design verification processes that recognise identity evolves rather than remaining fixed. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a clear and feasible change that targets verification in Source B.  

Award an additional <marks>1 mark</marks> for an explicit link to the idea that identity is dynamic for each suggested change, up to a maximum of <marks>4 marks</marks>.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Changes must be consistent with the verification design described in Source B. Responses that only list changes without linking to dynamic identity can earn a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>.</note>


**Sample answer**


UniSphere could improve its verification system by allowing multiple display names with clear context-specific rules, so students can use a chosen name in forums while retaining a legal name for formal processes. This recognises that people present their identities differently depending on the situation.  

The platform could also introduce a supportive update workflow for changing names or photos that avoids punitive language. This would acknowledge that identities and appearances change over time and that inconsistency does not imply dishonesty.

parts.content: Compare what Source C and Source D indicate about the effects of UniSphere’s “Verified Student” badge on inclusion and participation.
parts.markscheme:
**Compare (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Source C reports that 37 percent of students experience a two-tier feeling, suggesting reduced inclusion for unverified users, while Source D also highlights exclusion created by verification systems. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source D argues that the optional badge becomes effectively required in valuable spaces, reinforcing gatekeeping, whereas Source C presents this as an unintended outcome rather than a design critique. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C shows that 48 percent of students report increased trust and reduced impersonation, while Source D questions whether this trust comes at the cost of broader participation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source D suggests participation may shrink due to narrow identity templates, while Source C focuses more on participation gains for verified users. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C identifies friction such as photo mismatch flags and help tickets, while Source D frames these as structural burdens rather than minor technical issues. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Both sources recognise tensions between improved trust and the exclusionary effects of verification systems. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for effective comparisons that clearly use both Source C and Source D.  

Award a maximum of <marks>3 marks</marks> if only one source is used.  

Award a maximum of <marks>4 marks</marks> if the sources are discussed separately rather than comparatively.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses must explicitly compare similarities or differences between the two sources rather than treating them independently.</note>


**Sample answer**


Source C and Source D both address how the Verified Student badge affects inclusion and participation, but they emphasise different aspects. Source C reports that 37 percent of students experience a two-tier feeling, indicating reduced inclusion for those without verification. Similarly, Source D argues that the optional badge becomes effectively required in high-value spaces, reinforcing exclusion and gatekeeping.  

However, the sources differ in emphasis. Source C highlights that nearly half of students feel the badge increases trust and that impersonation incidents decrease, suggesting participation improves for some users. In contrast, Source D argues that participation may shrink overall because legitimacy becomes tied to compliance with a narrow identity template. Both sources acknowledge technical friction, but while Source C treats issues like photo mismatches as operational problems, Source D frames them as deeper structural risks tied to forced consistency and disclosure.

parts.content: To what extent do digital platforms like UniSphere shape people’s identities more than individuals shape their own digital identities? With reference to all the sources (A–D) and your own knowledge of the Digital Society course, consider the relationship between legal, account, and social identity.
parts.markscheme:
**To what extent (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Digital platforms shape identity through design features such as badges and access controls that influence how users present themselves. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Context collapse occurs when systems fail to display pronouns or language preferences consistently, flattening identity across spaces. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Platform architecture creates identity friction through cross-tool mismatches that constrain self-presentation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Legal identity is privileged when support staff can view legal names, prioritising institutional identity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Users show agency by choosing display names, with many differing from their legal names. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Optional verification reflects some user choice, though this choice is constrained by access pressures. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Impacts vary by risk and social position, with some users disproportionately burdened by verification. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Optional systems can function as coerced choices when tied to opportunity access. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Platforms set affordances, but users enact identity within those constraints. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Accountability involves transparent policies, privacy by design, and accessible correction paths. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Legal, account, and social identities interact in complex ways online. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Intersectionality shapes how different users experience platform identity controls. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to <marks>12 marks</marks> for a balanced evaluation that considers both platform influence and individual agency, uses all sources, and integrates relevant own knowledge.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses using only sources or only own knowledge can earn a maximum of <marks>8 marks</marks>. Full marks require synthesis and a justified judgement.</note>


**Sample answer**


Digital platforms like UniSphere strongly shape how people present themselves online, although individuals retain some agency within these systems. Platform design features such as the Verified Student badge act as legitimacy signals that influence how users are perceived and encourage conformity to certain identity templates. When pronouns or language preferences are not consistently displayed, identity can become flattened across contexts, demonstrating how technical design shapes self-presentation. The platform infrastructure also creates friction through cross-tool mismatches that limit how identities can be expressed.  

Platforms often privilege legal identity, as support staff access to legal names places institutional identity above chosen or social identity. This demonstrates the power platforms hold in defining which aspects of identity matter most. At the same time, users exercise agency by selecting display names, with many choosing names that differ from their legal records. Participation in verification is optional, showing some user choice, although this choice is constrained when access to valuable opportunities depends on verification.  

The effects of these systems are uneven. Students with higher disclosure risks may experience greater burdens, highlighting the importance of intersectionality. Overall, platforms set the structure and limits of identity presentation, while individuals act within those boundaries. The extent to which platforms shape identity is therefore significant, but not total, and meaningful accountability requires designs that recognise identity as dynamic and context-dependent.

parts.content: Identify two types of machine learning and describe their uses.
parts.markscheme:
### Supervised Learning
Supervised learning is a type of machine learning where algorithms are trained on labeled data. This means that the input data is already categorized or associated with the correct output. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Supervised learning is used for tasks such as:
- Image recognition/classification (identifying objects, people, etc. in images)
- Spam detection (classifying emails as spam or not spam)
- Fraud detection (identifying fraudulent transactions)
- Predictive modeling (predicting future outcomes based on past data)

### Unsupervised Learning
Unsupervised learning is a type of machine learning where algorithms are trained on unlabeled data to find patterns or relationships within the data. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Unsupervised learning is used for tasks such as:
- Clustering (grouping similar data points together)
- Anomaly detection (identifying unusual or outlier data points)
- Dimensionality reduction (reducing the number of features in data)
- Association rule learning (finding relationships between different data attributes)

- Supervised learning for image recognition involves training a model on a large dataset of labeled images $\blacksquare$ <marks>M1</marks>

$$
\text{Model is trained to map input images } \rightarrow \text{ to correct output labels}
$$

- During training, the model learns to recognize patterns/features associated with each label $\blacksquare$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Once trained, the model can classify new unseen images by identifying relevant patterns $\blacksquare$ <marks>M1</marks>

<note>Maximum 3 marks if steps are missing or unclear</note>

- Finding patterns/anomalies in data without being trained on labeled examples <marks>1 mark</marks>
$$
\begin{align*}
&\text{It looks for unusual behavior/outliers in transactions} \\
&\text{compared to normal/expected patterns}
\end{align*}
$$

- Using clustering algorithms to group similar transactions together <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transactions very different from clusters could indicate potential fraud <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Mention specific unsupervised learning techniques like k-means clustering, anomaly detection etc.</examTip>

parts.content: Evaluate the challenges of using machine learning for high-stakes decisions, such as in financial fraud detection, considering both accuracy and accountability.

### Accuracy challenges

### Accountability challenges

$$
\text{Lack of transparency} \implies \text{difficulty in assigning accountability}
$$
- Errors or biases in models could lead to unfair decisions with serious consequences <marks>1 mark</marks>
<commonMistake>Failing to consider both accuracy and accountability challenges</commonMistake>

### Other relevant points

questions.specification:
**Social media and political tension**

National governments sometimes block websites at a time of political tension. One example was in 2011, when a government prevented its citizens’ access to _Twitter_, _Facebook_ and _YouTube_. Images on these websites were considered inappropriate, with the potential to cause hatred, violence and political unrest in that country. The national government gave direct orders to the internet service providers (ISPs) to block access to these sites for four hours. The aim was to give enough time for site officials to remove the offending images from their websites.

Despite these attempts by the government, some citizens were still able to access these images using proxy servers.

Many people, such as academics, believe that this approach is not appropriate and that governments should focus on the education of their citizens so that they are able to make informed decisions about how to react to potentially offensive information on websites and social media.

parts.content:
Many schools block access to social networking websites like _Twitter_, _Facebook_ and _YouTube_. However, other schools are investigating two different options:

*   Monitoring the network to view what websites the students are viewing.
*   Giving different ages of students different levels of access to social media sites.

Analyse these **two** options.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Monitoring the network:**

*   It prevents cyberbullying – keep students safe (values).
*   It teaches students responsibility – digital citizenship (values).
*   It protects students from sharing inappropriate information and images about themselves (values).
*   It identifies any objectionable/inappropriate content that students can access on these sites.
*   It gathers data/information for future policies for the use (or not) of social media.
*   Students of all ages would have access to the same websites, regardless of age – younger students could have access to objectionable/inappropriate content for their age.
*   Privacy of students – school would have access to see what the students are viewing.

**Giving different ages different levels of access:**

*   Some websites have age restrictions – should only be accessed if age appropriate (values).
*   Younger students should not be exposed to objectionable/inappropriate content that is acceptable for older students.
*   Schools have a responsibility to ensure the use of the network is appropriate to various ages.
*   Younger students may not be able to differentiate between true intentions of online friendships (expression).
*   Older students need to learn responsible digital citizenship and take responsibility for their actions (values, trust).
*   Awareness of the benefits and drawbacks of social media sites is gained as the student matures (values).
*   Blocking sites by age could cause issues – blocking content that is needed by older students granting different levels of access may be more cost-effective than implementing an ongoing monitoring system (power, values).

  
**Keywords:** home, online, social, relationships, responsibility, trust, software applications, apps, objectionable content, change, expression, identity, power, values, ethics

parts.content:
Many citizens have raised concerns about the surveillance of their web browsing history or the censorship of selected websites by their national government.

To what extent is it appropriate for national governments to use surveillance and censorship to control citizens’ access to websites?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Reasons that surveillance and censorship to control citizens’ access to websites is acceptable:**

*   It can keep children from being victims of cyberbullying, sex trafficking and pornography, objectionable/inappropriate content.
*   It can control/monitor various illegal activities (values).
*   It helps strengthen national security with laws against hacking and impose large fines and punishments (values).
*   It can lessen the incidence of identity theft.
*   Governments may be best informed to determine what may, or may not, be appropriate for their citizens (values).

**Reasons that surveillance and censorship to control citizens’ access to websites is not acceptable:**

*   It removes citizens’ freedom of expression.
*   It can be used to keep information from citizens that may infringe on their privacy and/or anonymity (trust).
*   It can be expensive for the government or may require too many resources for the benefits it brings (systems).
*   It may accidentally block sites that should not be blocked (systems).
*   Knowledge is power.
*   It can be used to identify people who oppose the government (values, expression).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** Automation, connectivity, change, power, systems, values, ethics, machine learning, accountability, transparency, autonomy, surveillance

parts.content: Evaluate the role of sorting algorithms in promoting certain products over others, considering both benefits and ethical concerns for sellers.
parts.markscheme:
### Evaluate the role of sorting algorithms in promoting certain products over others, considering both benefits and ethical concerns for sellers.

Sorting algorithms can be used to promote certain products over others, which has both benefits and ethical concerns for sellers.

**Benefits:**

- Improved user experience: Sorting algorithms can display the most relevant or popular products first, making it easier for customers to find what they are looking for. This enhances the overall shopping experience. <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Increased sales: By promoting certain products, sorting algorithms can drive more sales for those items, benefiting the sellers of those products. <marks>2 marks</marks>

**Ethical Concerns:**

- Unfair competition: Sorting algorithms can give an advantage to larger sellers or those who pay higher fees, making it difficult for smaller sellers to compete and gain visibility. This can lead to a monopolistic market. <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Bias and manipulation: Sorting algorithms can be designed to promote certain products based on factors other than relevance or popularity, such as sponsored listings or partnerships with specific sellers. This can be seen as manipulative and unethical. <marks>2 marks</marks>

<note>Maximum 4 marks for benefits and 4 marks for ethical concerns.</note>

<Marks>8 Total</Marks>

questions.specification:
### Source A
AI can be categorized into narrow AI, which performs specific tasks, general AI that hypothetically mimics human intelligence across domains, and superintelligence, an advanced concept that surpasses human capabilities. Today’s AI systems are largely narrow AI, such as recommendation algorithms or chatbots.

For instance, personal assistants like Siri and Alexa use narrow AI to provide information and services based on user queries.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/89df1be3-a438-4f1f-b477-eb0ff23490a5)

### Source B: Types and Applications of Machine Learning
Machine learning (ML) encompasses several approaches, including supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning. Supervised learning uses labeled data, unsupervised learning identifies patterns, and reinforcement learning involves an agent learning through trial and error.

For example, supervised ML is used in spam filtering, where emails are classified as spam or not based on pre-labeled examples.

### Source C: Artificial Neural Networks in Image Recognition
Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) are inspired by the human brain’s structure, with layers of interconnected nodes that process information. ANNs are integral to image recognition, enabling software to distinguish and categorize visual content.

For instance, facial recognition systems use ANNs to analyze features and match faces in photos to stored identities, with applications in security and social media.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ac1a19f9-1434-46aa-8d2b-ea652a6fd65e)

### Source D: The Evolution of Artificial Intelligence
The development of AI has progressed through several stages, from early rule-based systems to today’s advanced machine learning models. Key milestones include Alan Turing’s contributions in the 1950s, the rise of neural networks in the 1980s, and deep learning advancements in the 2000s.

For example, Turing’s concept of a thinking machine laid the groundwork for modern AI, sparking research that continues to evolve with applications in healthcare, finance, and beyond.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/36f0f73d-6391-47f7-9cfd-0a5fb72072f8)

parts.content: With reference to Sources A–D and your own knowledge, discuss the impact of AI and machine learning advancements on society. Consider both practical applications and ethical considerations, providing examples to support your response.
parts.markscheme:
Markscheme 5:

Practical Applications:
- AI and machine learning enable automation of tasks, increasing efficiency and productivity across industries <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Example: Recommendation algorithms used by e-commerce platforms to personalize product suggestions <marks>1 mark</marks>

- AI systems can process and analyze large amounts of data, providing insights and solutions in fields like healthcare and scientific research <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Example: Machine learning models used to analyze medical images and assist in disease diagnosis <marks>1 mark</marks>

- AI-powered virtual assistants and chatbots can provide personalized services and support, enhancing customer experiences <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Example: Siri and Alexa using natural language processing to understand and respond to user queries <marks>1 mark</marks>

Ethical Considerations:
- Concerns over privacy and data security, as AI systems rely on large amounts of personal data for training <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Example: Facial recognition systems raising privacy concerns due to potential misuse or unauthorized surveillance <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Potential for AI systems to perpetuate biases present in the training data, leading to unfair or discriminatory outcomes <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Example: AI-based hiring systems exhibiting gender or racial biases due to biased training data <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Ethical questions around the accountability and transparency of AI decision-making processes, particularly in high-stakes domains like healthcare or criminal justice <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Example: Concerns over the "black box" nature of some AI models, making it difficult to understand how decisions are made <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Candidates should provide a balanced discussion, considering both practical applications and ethical considerations, and support their points with relevant examples from the sources or their own knowledge.</note>

questions.specification:
_Google_’s DeepMind uses a machine learning algorithm and neural network to analyse data. _Google_ is now collaborating with the United Kingdom’s National Health Service (NHS) to develop systems that can improve the quality of health care.

Its first project is to create a system that can analyse digital scans of eyes and thus help doctors diagnose sight-destroying diseases in time for them to be treated. This is a complex problem for pattern recognition.

_Google_ needs large quantities of retinal scans to test the algorithm. Moorfields Eye Hospital in London has the largest collection of retinal scans in the world and will share them with _Google_ for free. The scans will be anonymized. Patients can opt out of sharing their data by emailing an NHS data protection officer. _Google_ will use the knowledge gained from this project to help create future software that can eventually be marketed to health care providers.

For this project, _Google_ needs to consider the needs and concerns of multiple stakeholders, including patients, doctors, and the NHS. _Google_ plans to hold four patient forums a year and set up a panel of independent reviewers to oversee what the company is doing. Ultimately, _Google_ hopes to make a profit from this technology.

*   Does this scheme cause harm to the patients? Is it ethical?

**Benefits for the NHS and its patients:**

*   _Google_ could provide free access to the system to the NHS vs other health providers.
*   The knowledge gained about preserving sight could be shared with doctors around the world, thereby benefiting many people at risk of losing their sight (values).
*   This data may be shared/mined, and other associations may be established that may lead to preventative health care in unrelated fields.
*   Patients may feel the benefits of sharing their data outweigh the negative implications of their data being shared (values).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** health, medical care, medical diagnostics, data, privacy, anonymity, algorithm, change, power, systems, values

parts.content:
**How to appropriately interact in an online environment**

Increasing numbers of parents are claiming digital technologies that monitor online activities are the best way to protect their children from offensive content. A number of companies, such as _mSpy_ and _Qustodio_, have developed online monitoring apps.

Other parents claim it is not necessary to monitor their children’s online behaviour, preferring to encourage their children to develop the knowledge and skills to make appropriate choices.

Parents can consider two different interventions to ensure their children use the online environment appropriately:

1.  Monitor their children’s online behaviour using apps
2.  Encourage their children to develop the knowledge and skills to use the online environment and make appropriate choices.

With reference to real-life scenarios, evaluate these two interventions.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Intervention 1: Online monitoring (claim)**

**Advantages of monitoring online behaviour using apps:**

*   Safety from exposure to inappropriate/offensive/objectionable content and interactions with people that have not been approved by the parent (online predators, etc).
*   Monitor interactions between the child’s social groups (e.g., online bullying / transparency).
*   Monitor time usage to ensure the child is not spending too much time online.

**Disadvantages of monitoring online behaviour using apps:**

*   Children may not know that their parents monitor their messages and online activities, which can be detrimental to trust and relationships within the family (ethics, values).
*   Trust issues from parental control may lead to the child seeking other online avenues behind their parents’ backs. Where does the power lie?
*   Children have a right to privacy, which these apps refute and deny (ethics, values).
*   Younger children may need to be protected, but teenagers may feel they have a right to exchange messages in confidentiality (transparency) with other people without their parents spying on them.
*   The use of apps could involve data being sent to the app developers. which potentially introduces new privacy issues (transparency).
*   The apps can be costly.
*   Does not prepare the child for later life.

**Intervention 2: Developing online knowledge and skills (counter-claim)**

**Advantages of encouraging the development of online skills (claims supporting the intervention):**

*   Develops lifelong skills/values that stay with the child into adulthood.
*   Promotes trust between parent and child (ethics, values).

**Disadvantages of encouraging the development of knowledge and online skills:**

*   May be inappropriate for younger children, who are not mature enough to make appropriate choices in the complex online environment (acceptability, ethics, values).
*   Developing skills/\[ethical\]values is a learning curve and mistakes may still be made, the consequences of which may be the safety of the child (i.e., online predators/cyberbullying), security (i.e., downloading a virus or malware), or cost (i.e., in-app purchases).
*   The online environment is constantly changing, and parents would have to ensure that they are up to date with good safety practices to ensure they are giving the best advice (ethics, values).

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts.  
  
_

**Keywords:** home, online, health, social, family, relationships, trust, advice, software applications, apps, offensive, inappropriate, objectionable, content, change, expression, transparency, identity, power, ethics, values
